query: effectiveness of ADAM questionnaire in diagnosing partial androgenic deficiency in aging men

retrieved_docs.title: Male Hypogonadism -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The Androgen Deficiency in Aging Males (ADAM) test can be a useful initial step in diagnosing male hypogonadism. It consists of a 10-item questionnaire intended to identify men exhibiting signs of testosterone deficiency. [18] However, some guidelines, such as the American Urological Association (AUA) Guideline on Testosterone Deficiency, do not currently recommend using validated questionnaires. [2] This is because questionnaires should not be substituted for a full patient evaluation with appropriate laboratory testing. Even validated questionnaires have highly variable reported sensitivities and specificities and are not a reliable alternative to formal laboratory testing. [2] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Male Hypogonadism -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level should be drawn in all patients with hypogonadism older than 40 years before initiating therapy. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Androgen Replacement -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Hypogonadism is diagnosed when the morning serum testosterone level is less than 300 ng/dL. However, clinical judgment is required when diagnosing hypogonadism in a patient with testosterone levels in the normal range but persistent testosterone deficiency symptoms. [5] In senior men, one should aim for testosterone levels between 500 and 800 ng/dL, while young adults should aim for testosterone levels between 600 and 900 ng/dL.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate-Specific Antigen -- Etiology and Epidemiology -- General Recommendations for Prostate-Specific Antigen Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Routine screening should be discontinued in men with a life expectancy of 10 years or less based on comorbidities or by the age of 75.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Dihydrotestosterone -- Function -- Adult
retrieved_docs.content: DHT does not play a significant role in the normal physiology of adults. The most notable effects are prostate enlargement and male pattern hair loss as they age. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Dihydrotestosterone -- Clinical Significance -- Androgen Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone is the primary hormone used in androgen-deficiency states like male hypogonadism, androgen deficiency of severe illness, androgen deficiency of aging, and microphallus in infancy. DHT has also been proposed as a treatment for androgen deficiency as it is pure androgen and does not convert to estrogen. A potential advantage of DHT over testosterone is the reported and seemingly paradoxically muted effects of DHT on prostate growth. The decreased impact of DHT on the prostate gland of humans may be due to the decrease in intraprostatic estradiol levels. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Female Sexual Interest and Arousal Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Off-Label Medication Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone: Available data show that testosterone treatment is effective in menopausal women with sexual desire dysfunction or decreased libido. [48] Evidence is mixed concerning the use of testosterone in premenopausal women, and further studies are needed for validation of its effectiveness. Most practice guidelines concerning testosterone supplementation for FSIAD in women do not recommend testing testosterone levels because no age-specific normal values for testosterone in women have been established. A common approach to treatment with testosterone is to individualize the dosing to resolve the symptoms while keeping adverse events to a minimum. [24] Oral and intramuscular preparations of testosterone are not recommended for women as they result in high fluctuations in levels. Similarly, subcutaneous implants are also not recommended due to the inability to titrate doses. Compounded testosterone cream is also not recommended because of the inability to regulate its concentration. Transdermal formulations, including patches, gel, cream, or spray, are preferred for use in women. Hand washing should be done after applying formulations to the upper thigh, back of the calf, or buttocks. The starting dose of testosterone gel for women is 5 mg/day (0.5 mL), which is one-tenth the starting dose used in men. Small increases can be made up to 10 mg/day (1.0 mL) if needed. [24] Baseline lipid panel and liver function testing should be initially obtained and monitored yearly with the use of testosterone therapy in women. Clinical benefits may be seen in 6 to 8 weeks, but 12 weeks of treatment may be needed to see maximum results. [24] Adverse events include acne, deepening of the voice, alopecia, adverse effects on lipid profile, and abnormal hair growth. On the other hand, testosterone is likely protective against the development of breast cancer. Therapy should be discontinued after 6 months if symptoms are not improved. Further studies on the long-term use of testosterone, adverse events, and metabolic and cardiovascular outcomes are needed. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Androgen Replacement -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients who may benefit from androgen replacement therapy based on clinical indications and assessment of hormone levels. Differentiate different types of androgen replacement therapies, such as testosterone preparations, and understand their pharmacokinetics, dosing, and administration routes. Assess the therapeutic response to androgen replacement therapy by regularly monitoring hormone levels, symptom improvement, and potential adverse effects. Select the optimal formulation, dosage, and delivery method of androgen replacement therapy based on patient preferences, adherence, and clinical considerations. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer Screening -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In men aged 55 to 69 years, the US Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) states that the decision to undergo periodic PSA screening should be an individual one after a thorough upfront discussion between the physician and patient regarding the risks, benefits, and limitations of such screening incorporating the patient's values and preferences. Recommends against routine PSA screening in men aged 70 and older. This is because the potential survival benefits from treatment are statistically minimal, and they do not outweigh the significant adverse effects and health issues that the average man in this age group may experience due to treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Androgen Replacement -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists (AACE) issued a guideline in response to the 2015 FDA labeling requirement on cardiovascular risk. The guideline stated that there is no compelling evidence that testosterone therapy increases cardiovascular risk. [28] On the other hand, testosterone deficiency correlates with falls, sarcopenia, frailty, osteopenia, and osteoporosis. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Male Hypogonadism -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: There are conflicting trials on the cardiovascular risks of testosterone, most notably the TOM (Testosterone in Older Men) trial and the TEAAM (Testosterone's Effects on Atherosclerosis Progression in Aging Men) trial. [69] [70] The TOM trial found that applying testosterone gel daily after 6 months was associated with increased cardiovascular events. [69] The TOM trial involved a sample of 209 men without monitoring serum testosterone levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Androgen Replacement -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacists should verify dosing, check for potential interactions, and provide thorough counseling for drug administration, which will vary depending on the treatment modality and formulation chosen.

retrieved_docs.title: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Exclusion of Defects in Testosterone Synthesis
retrieved_docs.content: 5-alpha-reductase deficiency: Differentiation of CAIS and PAIS from 5-alpha-reductase is done by analyzing the ratio of testosterone to DHT in serum. In patients with steroid 5-alpha-reductase 2 deficiency, the production of DHT decreases, and the plasma ratio of testosterone to DHT increases. In most girls/boys with PAIS, the production of DHT and the ratio of testosterone to DHT is normal. Women with CAIS may have secondary 5-alpha-reductase deficiency due to a decreased mass of the urogenital tract tissues, which normally produce DHT.

retrieved_docs.title: Androgen Replacement -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Initial laboratory testing should include 2 early morning (8 AM to 10 AM) serum testosterone measurements. In addition, certain studies should be ordered to rule out secondary hypogonadism if both measurements are low. Further testing includes FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone), LH (luteinizing hormone), prolactin, TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone), complete blood count (CBC), PSA (prostate-specific antigen) for men older than 40 years, and a comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1462
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone propionate is contraindicated in patients with hyperplasia of the prostate, because its androgenic activity can exacerbate prostatic enlargement and worsen the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1367
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and estrogens and Androgens and female sex hormones in combination with other drugs are both classified as types of androgens and female sex hormones in combination, indicating that therapeutic regimens that combine androgenic agents with female sex hormones can be administered either as a straightforward androgen‑estrogen mixture or as a more complex formulation that also includes additional pharmacologic agents. Recognizing these subcategories helps clinicians tailor hormone therapy to achieve the desired hormonal balance while addressing the need for adjunctive drugs in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Androgens are a class of steroid hormones that serve as male sex hormones, and nandrolone is a specific androgenic anabolic steroid within this group, known for its therapeutic use in conditions requiring anabolic effects.

Overall, the hierarchical organization shows that agents targeting BPH, erectile dysfunction, and related urological conditions are grouped under urologicals within the genito‑urinary and sex hormone system, with mechanism‑based subclasses (alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists, testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors, phosphodiesterase‑5 inhibitors) defining therapeutic use, physiological effects, and safety considerations, while organic nitrates serve cardiac indications with specific contraindications and vasodilatory actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2534
retrieved_docs.content: ANABOLIC AGENTS FOR SYSTEMIC USE are classified as synthetic androgens, which are man‑made compounds that mimic the biological activity of natural male sex hormones such as testosterone. As synthetic androgens, these agents are administered systemically to produce anabolic effects—including enhanced protein synthesis, muscle growth, and bone density—and are employed in therapeutic settings such as hormone‑deficient states, certain anemias, and muscle‑wasting disorders. Their synthetic nature also confers potential androgenic side effects, necessitating careful medical oversight during use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2933
retrieved_docs.content: Androgeny and the class of compounds known as androgens are both classified as forms of nandrolone, a synthetic anabolic‑androgenic steroid. As variants of nandrolone, they share the characteristic hormonal activity of this compound, which includes promoting protein synthesis, increasing muscle mass, and exerting androgenic effects. Recognizing androgeny and androgens as types of nandrolone helps clarify their pharmacologic profile and informs clinical decisions regarding their therapeutic use, potential side‑effects, and regulatory considerations.

retrieved_docs.content: risk factor for chronic kidney disease, cerebrovascular injury, autonomic dysregulation, prostatic disorders, and electrolyte disturbances. Effective management therefore requires an integrated approach that addresses blood‑pressure control with appropriate alpha‑1 antagonists, monitors for neurologic and renal complications, employs targeted therapies such as TURP for prostate enlargement, avoids contraindicated agents like testosterone propionate, and incorporates routine screening (التحصّي) for male genital disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and estrogens are classified as members of the broader hormone group that includes estrogens and testosterone, reflecting their role as sex steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Other male genital disorders are considered a subcategory of prostate disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) and are linked to the practice of screening (التحصّي). Recognizing that other male genital disorders fall under the broader category of prostate disorder and are associated with screening emphasizes the importance of appropriate diagnostic evaluation in the management of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 769
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and estrogens is identified as a member of the broader group estrogens / testosterone, indicating that this combination belongs to a category that includes both estrogenic and testosterone components. At the same time, Androgens and estrogens is classified as a type of androgens and female sex hormones in combination, reflecting its role as a therapeutic or physiological mixture of male and female sex hormones. The entity estrogens / testosterone is described as part of estrogeny, signifying that this group contributes to overall estrogenic activity within the body. Furthermore, Androgens and female sex hormones in combination with other drugs is recognized as a subclass of androgens and female sex hormones in combination, showing that when these hormones are administered together with additional pharmacologic agents they remain within the same overarching category of combined androgen‑female hormone therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2470
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders. Both belong to the class of synthetic conjugated estrogens and are indicated for alleviating the hormonal deficiencies and associated symptoms of menopause, such as vasomotor instability, urogenital atrophy, and bone loss. Their use underscores the role of estrogen replacement therapy in managing the clinical manifestations of menopause.

retrieved_docs.content: Udenafil and vardenafil are both classified as drugs used in erectile dysfunction, belonging to the therapeutic category of medications indicated for the treatment of this condition.
